item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So my two cents is that this thing is basically an Adderall box with some… odd effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but the execution is… Well, unfun. Needs to be more fun. The basic premise is solid, as it starts with a cliche then adds interesting twists, but they're underwhelmingly used and the show-don't-tell has too much tell, like the fact that after the interview the video transcript conveys next to no dissenting or new information aside from the face distortion, which isn't enough to push it into +100 territory, I'm afraid.

Downvoted to push it towards a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. The idea is interesting to me, just a couple of technical issues I see.

+ Suggested edits
- Suggested edits

The following interview has been deemed notable due to its content.

This sentence suggests a single interview log; however, there are two interview logs that follow.

D-14683, a former paralegal, was chosen for testing with SCP-2291. To test the long-term effects of SCP-2291 on subjects similar to the employees of █████████ Corporate Solutions.

These two sentences should be one. The second sentence feels weird reading, and from context clues, appears to be an incomplete sentence. The question "What is used to test long term effects…" should be answered here.

He found dead in his cell 4 months and 12 days after the above interview.

Should be "He was found dead…".

Keep up the good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Direct physical contact with SCP-2291 is to be considered a Delta-class cognitohazard.

A cognitohazard is something that's dangerous to perceive or know about. If the box affects people who have touched it, that's not really based on perception or knowledge- you could be affected if you bumped into it in the dark without knowing what it was, for instance, or you could perceive the presence of the box by touch but not be affected because you were wearing gloves.

On a related note, if you can handle the box safely by wearing gloves, then the "you must use D-Class to handle it" part of the containment procedures is kind of overkill; compare to the procedures on SCP-2208, which similarly Should Not Be Touched Ever.

That said, I like the object. Things that really grabbed me…

  1. The corporate drones aren't the usual Stepford Smilers (unsettlingly excited about their work and uninterested in anything else); it's more like they're stereotypical cubicle farm workers with their wires crossed. They feel like human beings when they're at work and are just going through the motions the rest of the time, instead of vice versa.
  2. The "fun" word replacement. Altering someone's vocabulary but not the ideas behind it, so that the person clearly knows what they want to say but can't express it, is creepy as hell and you've used it to good effect.
  3. The imagery in the last addendum, and the way it implies something more going on that we really don't understand- not just "where did the box come from?" but also "how did 2291-B get everyone to expose themselves like that?" and "if 2291-B can do that to people on their own, what do they need the box for?".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Corporate slave box isn't a terrible idea, but the article does nothing with it. There is precisely nothing in this article that someone who has been on this site for a while couldn't guess from the three word description above.

Actually, the distortion on the video irked me, because there was ko good reasom for it to be present.

I would like to see this rewritten; not everything benefits from "more backstory/narrative," but this could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your Addenda got me. Very creative. Nice work
I can safely say it has earned my upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool, I guess we'll see how this does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am digging this and the overall fun tone of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, you can feel free to rewrite if you want. I kinda want to explore other ideas anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Correct. In the episode, one character is cloned numerous times, and her clones bounce around shouting "Fun fun fun!" for the duration. Hence, the mental link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to thank you for your help with my revisions, by the way; you really improved this one a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Return of the fun box…

Well, I think that I am starting to look at this as not quite how I would write it, since I have had a concept for a rewrite since the first version, which isn't a fair place to review from. I think I am going to novote on that basis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's an episode of My Little Pony: Friendship is Magic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At the end of the day, the object is just a thing that makes you go crazy and eventually you kill yourself. Sure, there is a story… Kinda, but what you are supposed to take away from it, if anything, is lost to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what that is, but thanks for the input! I'll work on the video log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All I can think of is Too Many Pinkie Pies. <.<

My one actual criticism is that the video transcript is confusing. It doesn't seem to show off the effects of the box beyond the compulsion to touch it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got it! I also added a reference to Naloxone, which is a endorphin-blocking drug used to help treat people who OD on opiates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice idea. I won't vote right now, but in the sake of sounding more professional, how about describing happiness as endorphins? Endorphins are the chemicals responsible for a 'positive' reaction, therefore referencing the change in endorphins would apply a quantity of realsim.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of want to like this, but it's just not quite doing it. It's well done technically, and the interviews and Document 2291-A almost have me upvoting (the imagery in the latter has a nice creep factor). What its missing is that impossible-to-define "something" that pushes a work from "not bad" to "interesting".

Two questions/suggestions that might spark some brainstorming: "Why fun, and not any other word?" and "Assume that this wasn't created to exploit employees. What else could have been the intent, and was this the intended outcome?".

Edit: after some consideration, upvoted.
